call_brief: Boaz, John and Prakash from DealHub, along with Acumatica team members, discussed DealHub's quote-to-revenue capabilities, focusing on Use Case One which involved a partner resale scenario with complex billing and revenue recognition. call_summary: Use Case 1 Overview: John began by walking through Use Case 1, a PCS contract with partner build and an ISV SKU, demonstrating how orders, billing schedules, payment terms, operating entities, custom fields, partner margins, and line item discounts are handled, leading to an invoice preview. 2. Invoice Generation Methods: Three methods for invoice generation were presented: one-off (manual per subscription), bulk runs (across multiple subscriptions/entities), and automated runs (scheduled generation and posting/drafting). 3. ERP Integration & Entity Management: Elena inquired about multi-tenant ERP integration. John clarified that one DealHub tenant integrates with one ERP instance, which can manage multiple subsidiaries or branches, and DealHub supports native entity management. 4. Three ERP Integration Options: John detailed three ERP integration options: 1) DealHub as the full quote-to-revenue platform, sending only detailed journal entries to ERP; 2) DealHub solely as a billing calculation engine, sending invoices to ERP for AR management; and 3) a mixed approach where DealHub handles more AR functions, syncing transactions, but ERP still books journal entries. 5. Journal Entry Detail: John demonstrated that journal entries are highly detailed, with each line item generating two entries (debit/credit) and tracking various financial components like tax, cash collection, and foreign currency. 6. Invoice Posting & AR Capabilities: John showed how to post an invoice, noting that an accounting period must be open. He also mentioned DealHub's capabilities as an AR subledger, including voiding invoices, applying payments/credits, and reporting on AR aging, dunning, and tax. 7. Revenue Recognition Reporting: John demonstrated DealHub's reporting engine, leveraging Looker for out-of-the-box and customizable dashboards, including revenue waterfalls, and the ability to download raw data. 8. Revenue Recognition Rules Setup: John explained that revenue recognition rules are set up by entity/branch, sourced from the order, and offer three methods: ratable over time, event-based (e.g., percentage of completion), and point-in-time (e.g., 100% at start date). 9. Handling Complex Revenue Scenarios: The team discussed the challenge of splitting a single product SKU into multiple revenue schedules (e.g., 80% upfront, 20% ratable, or ISV royalties), noting that DealHub's current model is one order line item to one revenue schedule, and enhancements for SSP (Stand-alone Selling Price) and allocation are in development. 10. Looker Licensing: Prakash confirmed that Looker, used for DealHub's BI tool and dashboards, is a white-label solution included as part of the DealHub contract and does not require a separate license purchase.

call_summary: Salesforce Integration & Playbook Overview: DealHub integrates with standard Salesforce objects (Opportunity, Quote, Account), reading and syncing information. The playbook guides sales reps through quoting with a questionnaire, dynamically pulling products and pricing based on answers. 2. Admin Configuration & Playbook Rules: The admin interface allows configuration of playbooks, product catalog, pricing, discounts, parameters, workflows, and output documents. Questions can be added with tooltips, various answer types, and rules for conditional appearance based on other selections or Salesforce data. 3. Dynamic Question Logic: Questions can be made rule-based, appearing only when relevant (e.g., based on product type selected) and offering different answer sets based on other factors like the number of users or sales team. 4. Admin Version Control: Admins work in a sandbox-like environment by duplicating the active playbook version, making changes, testing, and then activating the new version. This allows for agile updates and the ability to roll back to previous versions if needed. 5. Product Catalog Management: DealHub serves as the source of truth for the product catalog, syncing new products to Salesforce's standard product object. Products can be grouped using tags and assigned various attributes. 6. Flexible Pricing Models: Pricing can be defined with standard list prices, and influenced by multiple factors like duration and user tiers, configured through tables in "list price factors." This allows for complex pricing logic to be applied across products. 7. Discount Management: Discounts can be managed through channel partner programs (e.g., silver, gold, platinum) applied to specific product groups. Ad-hoc manual discounts are also possible, potentially triggering approval workflows. 8. Currency Management: DealHub can read exchange rates from Salesforce or maintain them directly within its "geofactors" or "currency factors" tables, allowing for different rates per product type and simplifying multi-currency quoting. 9. Audit Trail & Change Tracking: Every answer and change within DealHub is captured, providing a complete audit trail. While not a direct "before and after" comparison for free text, all changes and approval records are synced back to Salesforce for visibility and reporting. 10. Subscription Management: DealHub has its own dedicated subscription object, which is created upon quote submission. Existing subscriptions from Salesforce CPQ can be migrated during onboarding, providing a 360-degree view of customer contracts and subscriptions.

Approval Email Deficiencies: Approvers receive a poorly formatted financial report within approval emails, especially on mobile, leading to blind approvals and confusion. 9.
